So the c cells increase the bars! The G borders decrease the bars.
- c cells: increase bars (5→14→23→5→...)
- G borders: decrease bars (23→14→5, but 5→5)
Wait, but [21, 39] didn't change N (5→5), and [21, 45] did change N (5→14). This means there are two different c cells that affect the N bar:
- [21, 39]: c cell in left container (N container) - didn't change N
- [21, 45]: c cell in right container (also N container?) - changed N from 5 to 14
And similarly for Y:
- [38, 24]: c cell in left container (Y container) - didn't change Y
- [44, 24]: c cell in right container (also Y container?) - changed Y from 5 to 14
Wait, let me look at the c cell boundaries:

- c cell 12: [[19, 43], [19, 47], [23, 47], [23, 43]] - this is the right container border (also N container?)

- c cell 22: [[42, 22], [42, 26], [46, 26], [46, 22]] - this is the bottom container border (also Y container?)
- c cell 30: [[63, 44], [63, 63]] - this is the timer bar
So the c cells are the container borders (G cells), not the cells inside the containers. The containers have G borders, and clicking on the G borders toggles the bars.
Wait, but earlier I said the G borders decrease the bars. But now the c cells (which are the G borders) are increasing the bars.

- Bottom-left container: c cell 19 (rows 36-40, cols 22-26) - Y container

- Bottom-left: two containers stacked (G borders with Y shapes inside)

- Bottom-left: two containers stacked (Y containers)

- c cell 19: top Y container border
- c cell 22: bottom Y container border
Clicking on the c cells increases the bars:

<parameter=code>

    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 21, 'col': 45},  # c cell 12: N 14→23

])

seg = current_frame.segmentation

y_bars = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'Y' and 5 <= n['pixels'] <= 25]
print(f"Current: N={[n['pixels'] for n in n_bars]}, Y={[n['pixels'] for n in y_bars]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
